steps.title: Limit the supplement claim
steps.content: The study found that zoo chimpanzees' vitamin D levels were lower in autumn than in summer and appeared unaffected by oral vitamin D supplements. But the researchers immediately qualify that supplement result: supplementation was rare, highly varied, and poorly tracked. That weak record means the study cannot confidently settle whether better or more consistent supplementation would help during the low-UVB season.

steps.title: Choose the cautious conclusion
steps.content: A invents a claim that supplement effects are clearer in summer than in autumn, which the passage never suggests. C questions whether the autumn vitamin D measurements were accurate, but the problem is with supplementation records, not the measurements themselves. D makes supplementation the likely main cause of differences even though the data are too poor for that. B keeps the conclusion cautious: a possible autumn benefit from supplementation cannot be ruled out. The answer is B.
